topic: One Option (Abnormal / Developmental / Health / Human Relationships)
subject: psychology

items.why: Students reverse IV and DV. The IV is what you CHANGE (input); the DV is what you MEASURE (outcome). Mnemonic: 'I change the I.'

items.q: In Pavlov's experiment, what is the conditioned response?
items.correct: Salivation to the bell alone, after pairing with food.
items.misconception: Salivation to the food (which is automatic).
items.random_wrong: The dog ringing the bell itself.
items.why: Students confuse the unconditioned response (automatic salivation to food) with the conditioned response (learned salivation to bell). The conditioned response is the LEARNED one.

items.q: What is the difference between reliability and validity?
items.correct: Reliability is consistency; validity is whether it measures what it claims to.
items.misconception: They are essentially the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Reliability is for quantitative, validity for qualitative.
items.why: Students treat these as synonyms. A bathroom scale that adds 5kg every time is reliable (consistent) but not valid (wrong reading). You can be reliable without being valid.

items.q: The Robbers Cave study was conducted by:
items.why: Correct answer: Sherif. “Asch” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Buss (1989) surveyed how many cultures on mate preferences?
items.why: It's 37. “10” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did Hazan and Shaver publish adult attachment styles?
items.why: The real figure is 1987 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
